macro_segments.title: Introduction: The Mishnah's Structure as Le'chatchila and Be'di'avad, and the Role of Initial Sugya in Presenting Major Themes

micro_segments.title: The First Discussion on Le'chatchila and Be'di'avad as a Tool to Introduce Major Issues of Shechita
The Pattern of Opening Sugyot in Masechta: Linguistic or Marginal Questions as Vehicles for Introducing Central Themes

macro_segments.title: Wrapping Up the First Answer: The Tamei Person and the Laws of Tuma'at Meis; the Principle of Cherev ke-Chalal

micro_segments.title: Hai Tamei De-itma Be-mai: When Did the Tamei Person Become Tamei? The Distinction Between Tuma'at Meis and Tuma'at Sheretz
The Halakha of Cherev ke-Chalal: Metal Objects Retain the Same Level of Tuma as a Met, Explained Through Avi Avot Hatuma and Tsimtzum of Levels
First Solution: When the Tamei Person Became Tamei Through a Sheretz Rather Than a Met, He Is Only an Av, Not Avi Avot
Second Solution: The Knife as a Reed or Non-Metal Object; Braita on What Objects Can Be Used for Shechita (Rock, Glass, Reed)
Tosafot's Questions on the Requirement of a Kli for Shechita: Are These Objects Vessels for Tuma Laws? Parallels to Shechitat Kodashim

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Answer: The Samaritan (Kuti) as a Valid Shochet Under Supervision; Supervision Levels (Omeid al Gabav vs. Yotzei Ve-Nichnas) and the Test of Eating the Meat

micro_segments.title: The Question of the Kuti's Status and Reliability: Are Samaritans Jewish? Do They Keep Shechita Laws Properly? The Concern About Lifnei Iver
Abaye's Reading: The Mishnah Distinguishes Between Direct Supervision (Omeid al Gabav) for Le'chatchila and the Salvage Test (Eating the Meat) for Be'di'avad
The Salvage Mechanism: Cutting a Piece of Meat and Offering It to the Kuti; If He Eats, Shechita Is Valid; If He Refuses, It Remains Invalid
Exclusion of Cheresh, Shoteh, and Katan: Even With Direct Supervision, They Cannot Perform Shechita Be'di'avad; Concern About Pausing, Pressure, or Chiling
The Difficulty with the Final Phrase Vechulan Sheshachatu: Does It Refer to the Kuti or Cheresh Shoteh Katan?

macro_segments.title: Rava's Objection and Counter-Proposal: Yotzei Ve-Nichnas (Periodic Checking) as Sufficient Le'chatchila, Based on the Precedent of Yayin Nesech

micro_segments.title: Rava's Question: Why Does Abaye Demand Omeid al Gabav When the Mishnah on Wine Permits Yotzei Ve-Nichnas as Sufficient?
Rava's Distinction: The Case of Yayin Nesech Shows That Yotzei Ve-Nichnas Works; Response That This Applies Only Be'di'avad, Not Le'chatchila
Rava's Further Evidence and Abaye's Rejoinder: The Distinction Between Guarding (Shomer) and Processing (Shochet); Opportunity, Incentive, and the Severity of the Wrong
Rava's Re-Reading of the Mishnah: Yotzei Ve-Nichnas Is Sufficient Le'chatchila; the Case Where the Kuti Already Shechted (Ba Umatza Sheshachat) Requires the Salvage Test

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Answer: The Mumar (Habitual Violator Who Eats Neveilot); Removing Incentive by Pre-Checking the Knife

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mumar Category: Two Types (Mumar Le'Te'avon and Mumar Lehach'is) and Why the Neveilot Case Is Particularly Relevant to Shechita
The Case of Mumar Le'Te'avon: Checking the Knife Beforehand and Entrusting It to Him Removes the Incentive to Perform Incorrectly
If the Knife Was Not Pre-Checked: Checking It After the Fact; If the Knife Is Good, Shechita Is Valid; If Not, or If the Knife Cannot Be Checked, Shechita Is Invalid
Exclusion of Cheresh Shoteh Katan Even for the Mumar Case; The Concern Is Technical Competence, Not Just Honesty (Pressure, Pausing, Chiling)
Difficulty with Vechulan Sheshachatu: Is It Referring to the Mumar or Cheresh Shoteh Katan?

macro_segments.title: Ravina's Answers: The Criteria of Expertise (Mumcheh) and Experience (Muchzak); Knowledge vs. Practical Ability

micro_segments.title: First Version of Ravina: All Can Shecht If They Are Mumchin (Expert in the Law); Knowledge of Hilchot Shechita Is Essential
Testing Before and After: If Not Tested Before, Testing After Suffices; If He Knows the Laws, Shechita Is Valid Be'di'avad
Exception of Cheresh Shoteh Katan: Even If They Pass a Knowledge Test, They Cannot Perform Shechita; Concern Is Technical Performance, Not Abstract Knowledge
Vechulan Sheshachatu Under Ravina's Reading: Refers to Someone Who Is Not Tested as Mumchin; Direct Observation Suffices If He Cannot Be Tested Afterward
Second Version of Ravina: All Can Shecht If They Are Muchzakin (Experienced); Experience Even Without Formal Knowledge Testing
The Requirement of Witnessing Two or Three Prior Shechitot Without Fainting; Concern About the Shochet Fainting and Ruining the Shechita
Be'di'avad: If He Reports That He Did Not Faint and Performed Correctly, His Shechita Is Valid Even Without Prior Witnessing of Experience

macro_segments.title: Summary and Reconciliation: Why Each Amora Rejected Others' Approaches; The Debate Between Knowledge, Experience, and Assumptions

micro_segments.title: Why the First Answer (Tuma'ah) Was Rejected: The Tamei Case Is Not Relevant to Chullin and Does Not Require Tuma Concerns
Why Ravina's Approaches Were Rejected: The Assumption That Most Who Present Themselves to Shecht Know the Laws; We Do Not Require Formal Testing
The Rejection of the Fainting Concern: We Are Not Concerned That an Expert in the Law Will Faint; Knowledge Implies Practical Ability
Rav Ashi's Rejection of the Kuti Answer: Kutim Are Not True Converts (Gerei Arayot), So the Kuti Case Is Irrelevant
Abaye's Rejection of the Mumar Answer: He Does Not Hold That Pre-Checking the Knife Is Sufficient for a Mumar; Direct Supervision Is Required
Rava's Position on His Own Teaching: He Agrees That the Mumar Case Could Explain the Mishnah, But He Was Responding Polemically to Abaye's Supervision Requirements

topical_tags.term: Supervision: Omeid al Gabav (Direct) vs. Yotzei Ve-Nichnas (Periodic)
Tuma'at Meis and Avi Avot Hatuma
Shema Yish'hu, Shema Yidresu, Shema Yachlidu (Pausing, Pressure, Chiling)
Lifnei Iver (Not Putting Stumbling Block Before the Blind)
Rov Hametzuyim Eitzel Shechita Mumchin Hen (Most Who Perform Shechita Are Expert)
